rfect opportunity for box office guru Scott
Mendelson to offer his take on both the holiday movie race and the entire y=
ear that was. All yours, Scott=E2=80=A6

An array of disparate success stories=E2=80=94from
period horror gambles and animated sequels to an inter-gen biopic=E2=80=94m=
ade up for a lackluster summer and illustrated a new post-pandemic, post-st=
rike, post-#Barbenheimer paradigm in this strange new box office world.=C2=
=A0=C2=A0

Scott Mendelson ( https://puck.news/author/scott-mendelson/?utm_campaign=3D=
What+I%27m+Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F2%2F24%29&utm_content=3DWhat+I%27m+=

Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2812%2F24%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_sour=
ce=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )

So, the Christmas weekend box office total=E2=80=94$163 million=E2=80=94was=
n=E2=80=99t
entirely on par with the pre-pandemic 2019 sum of $197 million. But in a se=
ason without a single Star Wars- or Avatar-sized tentpole, the 14 percent d=
rop from the Before Times number may even be rosier than it initially appea=
rs. In a year billed as a Great Holding Pattern of sorts, marred by lingeri=
ng pandemic distortions and post-strike delays=E2=80=94a rebuilding year fo=
r the theatrical business and its Survive Until =E2=80=9925 mantra=E2=80=94=
this past weekend modeled a formula:
It wasn=E2=80=99t about a single, overperforming tentpole, but rather a sle=
w of thrillers, comedies, biopics, musicals, animated films, and horror mov=
ies, all performing solidly.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

Mufasa: The Lion King, the closest approximation to a blockbuster, out-earn=
ed its domestic debut, with $37 million in its second weekend compared to $=
35 million in its first, and is on track to crack $200 million
domestically. This performance aligns with the past four decades of mid- to=
 late December releases=E2=80=94films as huge as Avatar and as tiny as Mous=
e Hunt have traded smaller opening weekends for much larger opening-to-fina=
l multipliers amid the year-end moviegoing blitz.

=C2=A0

Disney=E2=80=99s approximately $200 million-budgeted follow-up won=E2=80=99=
t come anywhere near the $543 million domestic and $1.66 billion global fin=
ish of
the previous Lion King remake, in 2019, but that was never in the cards. Pr=
esuming it flirts with a $600 million worldwide total, Mufasa=E2=80=99s eve=
ntual box office will more closely resemble Aquaman and the Lost Kingdom ($=
433 million, or 62 percent below Aquaman=E2=80=99s $1.15 billion release) t=
han The Marvels and Joker: Folie =C3=A0 Deux, both of which barely earned $=
200 million despite their predecessors each topping $1 billion globally.

For your awards consideration Max Presents: The HBO Original HOUSE OF THE D=
RAGON. Following
King Aegon II=E2=80=99s coronation and the death of Queen Rhaenyra=E2=80=99=
s son, House Targaryen is divided in two. In King=E2=80=99s Landing, Aegon =
II rules from the Iron Throne, supported by Alicent and the Green Council, =
under the protection of the largest dragon in Westeros. From exile on Drago=
nstone, Queen Rhaenyra and her Black Council consider their next move. Civi=
l war looms as both sides seek support from the great houses of Westeros, u=
ltimately amassing armies =E2=80=94 and dragons. Don=E2=80=99t miss the ser=
ies Variety
calls =E2=80=9CTV=E2=80=99s GREATEST SPECTACLE=E2=80=9D. Now streaming on M=
AX.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

In the end, Mufasa is a middle-of-the-road
tentpole=E2=80=94think Paramount=E2=80=99s $440 million-grossing Gladiator =
II or Disney=E2=80=99s own $575 million-grossing Little Mermaid remake=E2=
=80=94that will offer a fine capstone to Bob Iger=E2=80=99s year, which fea=
tured $4 billion worldwide just from Inside Out 2, Deadpool & Wolverine, an=
d Moana 2.

=C2=A0

Meanwhile, the success of Focus=E2=80=99s non-franchise vampire flick,
Nosferatu, is even more illuminating. Robert Eggers=E2=80=99 R-rated remake=
 of F.W. Murnau=E2=80=99s 1922 silent film performed like a mainstream tent=
pole, with a $40 million Wednesday-Sunday launch, for several reasons: a st=
rong ensemble cast, a marquee filmmaker, an easy elevator pitch, good revie=
ws, and the promise of some holiday escapism. Focus tried something less co=
nventional, and now the industry has another genre of film that can success=
fully launch during
what has always been a robust period for multiple releases.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

The intergenerational biopic A Complete Unknown, which just passed $30 mill=
ion domestically on day eight, following a $23 million Wednesday-Sunday hol=
iday debut, was another exemplar of spreading the wealth. Whether or not it=
 holds firm over the early months of 2025, the James Mangold-directed drama
debuted with support from a solid combination of older moviegoers, who trea=
ted Bob Dylan as a marquee character, and younger fans, who just wanted to =
see Timoth=C3=A9e Chalamet talk like he swallowed a bee.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

The concurrent success of Nosferatu and A Complete Unknown, especially amid=
 the backdrop of a strong Thanksgiving featuring Wicked: Part One, Moana
2, and Gladiator II, demonstrates the renewed opportunity at the box office=
=2E Unlike the summer of #Barbenheim=
er, in 2023, Hollywood didn=E2=80=99t nee=
d to bank on an unprecedented pop culture event. And there may be more good=
 news to come. Relative to budget and expectation, there=E2=80=99s room for=
 Mufasa to continue building, and Nosferatu to demolish precedent for a lat=
e-December chiller. Babygirl, the Nicole Kidman-starring erotic thriller, a=
lso
performed well, auspiciously topping $10 million by day six.=C2=A0

A Happy End to a
Challenging Year
----------------

The overall total for the domestic box office in 2024 came in at
around $8.7 billion, down just 3 percent from last year=E2=80=99s Nolan-Rob=
bie-Cruise-adjusted $8.9 billion cume. Not only was the Christmas weekend o=
ff only 14 percent from the pre-pandemic era, but the frame amplified a dec=
idedly strong second half for the theatrical industry. After all, the first=
 five months of the year=E2=80=94acutely battered by strike delays and faci=
ng an oft-barren slate=E2=80=94were down 23 percent from 2023. But once the=
 movies showed up, from
Bad Boys: Ride or Die to Sonic the Hedgehog 3, the period from June through=
 December was up around 9 percent year over year.

=C2=A0

Of course, the total was still well below the $11 billion-plus annual hauls=
 that Hollywood grew accustomed to in the late 2010s. But paramount among t=
he reasons for tempered optimism here: The current predicament has as much =
to do with supply as demand.
Following the heights of the superhero era, Hollywood had to construct an e=
ntirely new playbook=E2=80=94discovering (or rediscovering) that, for insta=
nce, top-tier box office can result from big films aimed at women (and not =
just stereotypically male-centric genre films with a female lead). The stud=
ios behind The Fall Guy and Gladiator II were banking on women showing up t=
o watch Ryan Gosling and Paul Mescal, respectively. And the industry
didn=E2=80=99t need men to turn out for Blake Lively in It Ends With Us or =
Ariana Grande in Wicked: Part One. =C2=A0

=C2=A0

Yes, Disney lorded over its competition this past year, but studios offered=
 a veritable cornucopia of counterprogramming, whether by intent or post-st=
rike cupboard-cleaning necessity, or both. Besides the sky-high grosses for=
 Dune: Part Two and Wicked:
Part One, we saw good-to-great showings from smaller-scale, less convention=
al event films, like the aforementioned It Ends With Us and Longlegs, many =
of which helped fill in the gaps between periodically underwhelming tentpol=
es. After M. Night Shyamalan=E2=80=99s Trap underperformed in early August =
and Badlands outright tanked, Blake Lively=E2=80=99s blockbuster melodrama =
and Maika Monroe and Nicolas Cage=E2=80=99s thriller
mitigated the damage. The industry erred by resting October entirely on the=
 shoulders of one killer clown sequel, but Terrifier 3 was nonetheless up t=
o the challenge.=C2=A0

5921PUCK/B32813088.410105875;dc_trk_aid=3D602465610;dc_trk_cid=3D227506425;=

To state the obvious: Hollywood cannot survive by hoping every would-be
blockbuster performs at maximum potential. Having an Avatar or an Amazing S=
pider-Man 2 on deck is not enough. Studios and theater, alike, need an It=
=E2=80=99s Complicated and a Neighbors, too.=C2=A0

A Theatrical
Industry, If You Can Keep It
----------------------------

This new year has been pitched as the first time since 2020 that we
will see something approximating a pre-Covid slate of feature releases. Off=
hand, we=E2=80=99ve got a few more Marvel and DC movies, another Jurassic W=
orld, follow-ups to Zootopia and Avatar, and sequels to more recent franchi=
se successes like Wicked and M3GAN. There=E2=80=99s also plenty of optimism=
 for less franchise-minded likely breakouts, including the sure-to-be-contr=
oversial Michael Jackson biopic (Michael) and Ryan
Coogler=E2=80=99s Sinners, starring Michael B. Jordan.

=C2=A0

Alongside this return to semi-normalcy, distributors have come to recognize=
 a theatrical release as creating added value for streaming, rather than as=
 a rival channel. With a likely full slate, presuming Amazon somewhat compe=
nsates for the lack of a fully functioning 20th Century Studios, the likes =
of AMC and Cinemark can
finally do their thing without one hand tied behind their backs.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

Two of last year=E2=80=99s key patterns are instructive. Firstly, films of =
all shapes and sizes displayed post-debut staying power far above what woul=
d have been expected in pre-Covid times. For instance, Longlegs and The Fal=
l Guy earned out more than three times their respective opening weekends. T=
he hope is that,
even amid shrinking exclusivity windows, audiences are reacclimating to see=
ing a movie when they get around to it, and post-debut buzz can help long a=
fter opening weekend.

=C2=A0

At the same time, several titles earned more domestically than expected whi=
le posting only modest receipts overseas. Twisters earned $270 million in N=
orth America, but just $375 million worldwide. The family-skewing If
made $111 million domestically but only $190 million globally. Of course, g=
lobal earnings become less crucial if budgets are small and/or domestic gro=
sses are large enough. Wicked: Part One, for instance, cost $145 million an=
d might top $500 million stateside, so its current 67-33 domestic/overseas =
split isn=E2=80=99t exactly a catastrophe. With China rarely pitching in an=
d more Hollywood I.P.s skewing domestic in terms of interest and nostalgia,=
 budgets should reflect a global earnings split
closer to 50-50 than the pre-Covid standard 35-65 moving forward.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

The decline in once-dominant genres and studios=E2=80=99 reliance on past-t=
heir-prime brands and franchises are still cause for concern. That=E2=80=99=
s to say nothing of a generation more addicted to YouTube and video games t=
han to traditional longform entertainment=E2=80=94a challenge exacerbated b=
y an industry dedicated more to convincing kids that Indiana
Jones is still cool than to offering of-the-moment, youth-skewing franchise=
s like Five Nights at Freddy=E2=80=99s. And then there are the other macroe=
conomic challenges: The top six grossers of the year comprised 32 percent o=
f the annual theatrical revenue, up from 28 percent last year, continuing a=
 decade-long trend toward consolidation around a handful of all-encompassin=
g event movies. And that=E2=80=99s without even contemplating the unknowns =
at Paramount and WBD.

=C2=A0

Cautious optimism aside, it=E2=80=99s hard to picture the industry reboundi=
ng to 2000s and early 2010s levels in terms of ticket sales. Even the repor=
ted $2.2 billion-plus in expected=E2=80=94and long overdue=E2=80=94upgrades=
 to the multiplex experience are as much about maximizing revenue per custo=
mer as they are about attracting new business.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

However, the aspirational
Christmas box office should presage a continued and permanent theatrical re=
covery. The positive grosses and varied slate together approach a platonic =
ideal of what the movie theater is supposed to represent, both commercially=
 and culturally. Time will tell if it=E2=80=99s the start of a happily ever=
 after or a false-hope finale before a darker, grittier sequel.=C2=A0

=E2=80=8A

Thanks to Scott. I=E2=80=99ll be back on Monday to officially usher you
into 2025.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

See you then.=C2=A0

margin-bottom:0px;">Meanwhile, the success of Focus=E2=80=99s non-franchise=
 vampire flick,

 has another genre of film that can successfully launch during
what has always been a robust period for multiple releases.=C2=A0</p><p sty=

debuted with support from a solid combination of older moviegoers, who trea=
ted <strong>Bob Dylan</strong> as a marquee character, and younger fans, wh=

wallowed a bee.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p=
><p style=3D"margin:0;">The concurrent success of <em>Nosferatu </em>and<em=
> A Complete Unknown</em>, especially amid the backdrop of a strong Thanksg=
iving featuring <em>Wicked: Part One</em>, <em>Moana
2,</em> and <em>Gladiator II</em>, demonstrates the renewed opportunity at =
the box office. Unlike the summer of #Barbenheimer, in 2023, Hollywood didn=
=E2=80=99t need to bank on an unprecedented pop culture event. And there ma=
y be more good news to come. Relative to budget and expectation, there=E2=
=80=99s room for <em>Mufasa</em> to continue building, and <em>Nosferatu</e=
m> to demolish precedent for a late-December chiller. <em>Babygirl</em>, th=

argin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">The overall total for the domestic box office i=
n 2024 came in at
around $8.7 billion, down just 3 percent from last year=E2=80=99s <strong>N=
olan</strong>-<strong>Robbie</strong>-<strong>Cruise</strong>-adjusted $8.9=
 billion cume. Not only was the Christmas weekend off only 14 percent from =
the pre-pandemic era, but the frame amplified a decidedly strong second hal=
f for the theatrical industry. After all, the first five months of the year=
=E2=80=94acutely battered by strike delays and facing an oft-barren slate=
=E2=80=94were down 23 percent from 2023. But once the movies showed up, fro=
m
<em>Bad Boys:</em> <em>Ride or Die</em> to <em>Sonic the Hedgehog 3</em>, t=
he period from June through December was up around 9 percent year over year=
=2E</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bo=
ttom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:=
0;margin-bottom:0px;">Of course, the total was still well below the $11 bil=
lion-plus annual hauls that Hollywood grew accustomed to in the late 2010s.=
 But paramount among the reasons for tempered optimism here: The current pr=
edicament has as much to do with supply as demand.
Following the heights of the superhero era, Hollywood had to construct an e=
ntirely new playbook=E2=80=94discovering (or rediscovering) that, for insta=
nce, top-tier box office can result from big films aimed at women (and <em>=
not</em> just stereotypically male-centric genre films with a female lead).=
 The studios behind <em>The Fall Guy </em>and <em>Gladiator II </em>were ba=
nking on women showing up to watch <strong>Ryan Gosling </strong>and <stron=
g>Paul Mescal</strong>, respectively. And the industry
didn=E2=80=99t need men to turn out for <strong>Blake Lively </strong>in <e=
m>It Ends With Us </em>or <strong>Ariana Grande </strong>in <em>Wicked: Par=
t One. </em>=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p=
 style=3D"margin:0;">Yes, Disney lorded over its competition this past year=
, but studios offered a veritable cornucopia of counterprogramming, whether=
 by intent or post-strike cupboard-cleaning necessity, or both. Besides the=
 sky-high grosses for <em>Dune: Part Two </em>and <em>Wicked:
Part One, </em>we saw good-to-great showings from smaller-scale, less conve=
ntional event films, like the aforementioned <em>It Ends With Us </em>and <=
em>Longlegs</em>, many of which helped fill in the gaps between periodicall=
y underwhelming tentpoles. After <strong>M. Night Shyamalan</strong>=E2=80=

=3D"margin:0;">To state the obvious: Hollywood cannot survive by hoping eve=
ry would-be

<em>Amazing Spider-Man 2</em> on deck is not enough. Studios and theater, a=
like, need an <em>It=E2=80=99s Complicated</em> and a <em>Neighbors</em>, t=

<table class=3D"c4" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspaci=
ng=3D"0" role=3D"presentation" style=3D"mso-table-lspace:0pt;mso-table-rspa=
ce:0pt;word-break:break-word;"><tbody><tr><td class=3D"r" style=3D"padding-=
bottom:32px;"><div style=3D"color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-family:'In=
ter','Arial';font-size:17px;font-weight:400;letter-spacing:0px;=
line-height:150%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"m=
argin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">This new year has been pitched as the first tim=
e since 2020 that we
will see something approximating a pre-Covid slate of feature releases. Off=
hand, we=E2=80=99ve got a few more Marvel and DC movies, another <em>Jurass=
ic World</em>, follow-ups to <em>Zootopia</em> and <em>Avatar</em>, and seq=
uels to more recent franchise successes like <em>Wicked</em> and <em>M3GAN.=
</em> There=E2=80=99s also plenty of optimism for less franchise-minded lik=
ely breakouts, including the sure-to-be-controversial <strong>Michael Jacks=
on</strong> biopic (<em>Michael</em>) and <strong>Ryan
Coogler</strong>=E2=80=99s <em>Sinners</em>, starring <strong>Michael B. Jo=
rdan</strong>.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p sty=
le=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Alongside this return to semi-normalcy, =
distributors have come to recognize a theatrical release as creating added =
value for streaming, rather than as a rival channel. With a likely full sla=
te, presuming Amazon somewhat compensates for the lack of a fully functioni=
ng 20th Century Studios, the likes of AMC and Cinemark can
finally do their thing without one hand tied behind their backs.=C2=A0</p><=
p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;marg=
in-bottom:0px;">Two of last year=E2=80=99s key patterns are instructive. Fi=
rstly, films of all shapes and sizes displayed post-debut staying power far=
 above what would have been expected in pre-Covid times. For instance, <em>=
Longlegs</em> and <em>The Fall Guy</em> earned out more than three times th=
eir respective opening weekends<em>.</em> The hope is that,
even amid shrinking exclusivity windows, audiences are reacclimating to see=
ing a movie when they get around to it, and post-debut buzz can help long a=
fter opening weekend.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p=
><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">At the same time, several titles =
earned more domestically than expected while posting only modest receipts o=
verseas. <em>Twisters</em> earned $270 million in North America, but just $=
375 million worldwide. The family-skewing <em>If</em>
made $111 million domestically but only $190 million globally. Of course, g=
lobal earnings become less crucial if budgets are small and/or domestic gro=
sses are large enough. <em>Wicked: Part One</em>, for instance, cost $145 m=
illion and might top $500 million stateside, so its current 67-33 domestic/=
overseas split isn=E2=80=99t exactly a catastrophe. With China rarely pitch=
ing in and more Hollywood I.P.s skewing domestic in terms of interest and n=
ostalgia, budgets should reflect a global earnings split
closer to 50-50 than the pre-Covid standard 35-65 moving forward.=C2=A0</p>=
<p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;mar=
gin-bottom:0px;">The decline in once-dominant genres and studios=E2=80=99 r=
eliance on past-their-prime brands and franchises are still cause for conce=
rn. That=E2=80=99s to say nothing of a generation more addicted to YouTube =
and video games than to traditional longform entertainment=E2=80=94a challe=
nge exacerbated by an industry dedicated more to convincing kids that India=
na
Jones is still cool than to offering of-the-moment, youth-skewing franchise=
s like <em>Five Nights at</em> <em>Freddy=E2=80=99s</em>. And then there ar=
e the other macroeconomic challenges: The top six grossers of the year comp=
rised 32 percent of the annual theatrical revenue, up from 28 percent last =
year, continuing a decade-long trend toward consolidation around a handful =
of all-encompassing event movies. And that=E2=80=99s without even contempla=
ting the unknowns at Paramount and WBD.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;
margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Caut=
ious optimism aside, it=E2=80=99s hard to picture the industry rebounding t=
o 2000s and early 2010s levels in terms of ticket sales. Even the reported =
$2.2 billion-plus in expected=E2=80=94and long overdue=E2=80=94upgrades to =
the multiplex experience are as much about maximizing revenue per customer =
as they are about attracting new business.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;ma=
rgin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">However, the aspirationa=
l
Christmas box office should presage a continued and permanent theatrical re=
covery. The positive grosses and varied slate together approach a platonic =
ideal of what the movie theater is supposed to represent, both commercially=
 and culturally. Time will tell if it=E2=80=99s the start of a happily ever=
 after or a false-hope finale before a darker, grittier sequel.=C2=A0</p></=
div>
